About This Story

In this powerful story, Yuna is a Korean adoptee. She is adopted at four months old and grows up in America. Yuna learns the Korean language and visits Korea. She sees herself reflected in her culture. Yuna climbs a mountain to find her roots. This is an inspiring story about reconnecting with lost heritage and finding belonging.

Imagine you are four months old. You are taken from the country where you were born. You are flown across an ocean to a new country. You grow up in a place where no one looks like you. You forget the language your birth parents spoke. You forget the food, the customs, the sounds of home. You spend twenty-two years trying to be someone else. But then, one day, you decide to go back. You decide to learn the language you lost. You decide to reconnect with the heritage that was taken from you. This is the story of Yuna—a story of loss, longing, and reclamation.

Between 1950 and 2000, over 200,000 Korean children were adopted by families in other countries. Many of these children grew up as the only Asian person in their communities. They lost connection to their birth language, culture, and heritage. This story takes place during that time.
